pairs.question: Who was Alexander Morris and what sports did he play?
pairs.answer: Alexander Morris (1858–1918) was a New Zealand sportsman known for playing both rugby union and cricket. He represented Dunedin in rugby union and made one first-class cricket appearance for Otago during the 1884–85 season.

pairs.question: What was Alexander Morris's career in the banking industry?
pairs.answer: Morris began working in banking in 1874 by joining the Colonial Bank of New Zealand in Dunedin. He managed branches in Lawrence and later Invercargill, and after the bank's amalgamation into the Bank of New Zealand in 1895, he was promoted to inspector, eventually becoming the resident inspector in Wellington.

pairs.question: When and where did Alexander Morris make his only first-class cricket appearance?
pairs.answer: Morris played his sole first-class cricket match for Otago against Auckland at Lancaster Park in Christchurch over the New Year period of 1884–85. He scored two runs and did not take any wickets in the match.

pairs.question: How did Alexander Morris's career in the banking sector progress over time?
pairs.answer: Morris started at the Colonial Bank of New Zealand in Dunedin in 1874. He was later appointed manager of the Lawrence branch, then the Invercargill branch after the bank's merger. In 1895, he became an inspector, initially traveling to various branches before settling as the resident inspector in Wellington.

pairs.question: What personal details are known about Alexander Morris's life?
pairs.answer: Morris was born in Dunedin in 1858 and attended Otago Boys' High School. He married and had three children. He worked at the Bank of New Zealand until his death in 1918 at the age of 59, although he had been on sick leave for some months prior.

pairs.question: Why is Alexander Morris notable in New Zealand sports history?
pairs.answer: Morris is notable for being a multi-sport athlete in 19th-century New Zealand. His only first-class cricket match and his rugby union involvement for Dunedin highlight his contributions to early New Zealand sports, particularly during the colonial era.
